attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of altered bowel habits. She reports increasing constipation, narrow calibre stools, and occasional bright red blood when wiping. She has lost 4 kg over this period without trying. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as an accountant and has no family history of bowel cancer.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals mild tenderness in the left lower quadrant without a palpable mass. Digital rectal examination shows no masses but traces of blood on the glove. Blood tests reveal haemoglobin 98 g/L (115-165), mean cell volume 76 fL (80-100), iron 6 μmol/L (10-30).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs highly suspicious for colorectal cancer, specifically in the left colon. The combination of altered bowel habits, narrow calibre stools, weight loss, and iron deficiency anaemia in a patient over 50 years old necessitates prompt investigation.

Colonoscopy is the most appropriate next investigation as it allows direct visualisation of the entire colon and rectum, with the ability to take biopsies for histological confirmation. It is the gold standard investigation for suspected colorectal cancer.

Faecal occult blood test is a screening tool for asymptomatic individuals. In a patient with clear symptoms and signs of colorectal cancer, including visible rectal bleeding, this test would delay appropriate investigation and diagnosis.

Computed tomography scan may be useful for staging once cancer is confirmed, but it is not the first-line investigation for diagnosing colorectal cancer. It cannot provide tissue diagnosis and may miss small lesions.

Flexible sigmoidoscopy only examines the distal portion of the colon. While this might identify a descending colon tumour, a full colonoscopy is preferred to exclude synchronous lesions and examine the entire colon.

Iron studies would provide more detail about the iron deficiency but would not address the underlying cause. The patient already has documented iron deficiency anaemia and concerning symptoms that require direct investigation.

The pattern of symptoms (left-sided abdominal pain, altered bowel habits, narrow calibre stools) is typical for left-sided colon cancer, which tends to be more obstructing in nature compared to right-sided lesions. Right-sided lesions often present more insidiously with iron deficiency anaemia without obvious changes in bowel habits.
learningPoints: Left-sided colon cancers typically present with altered bowel habits and narrow calibre stools due to luminal obstruction.
Iron deficiency anaemia in the context of colorectal symptoms should prompt investigation for colorectal cancer.
Colonoscopy is the gold standard investigation for suspected colorectal cancer as it allows direct visualisation and biopsy.
references: Eng C, Yoshino T, Ruíz-García E, Mostafa N, Cann CG, O’Brian B, et al. Colorectal cancer. The Lancet. 2024 Jul 20;404(10449):294–310.
